raw_cv_text:
[CANDIDATE_NAME], PhD 
[EMAIL] ▪ [PHONE] 
https://www.linkedin.com/in/javier-rodriguez-g/ 

I  am  a  structural  and  molecular  biologist  with  7+  years  of  experience  in  protein  structure–function 
analysis, and biophysical and biochemical protein characterization. I am an expert in cryo-EM and X-ray 
crystallography with a proven record of optimizing the expression and purification of challenging protein 
complexes,  generating  high-resolution  structures,  and  collaborating  with  cross-functional  teams  in 
academia  and  industry.  Passionate  about  translating  molecular  insight  into  actionable  data  that 
accelerates drug target validation and lead optimization. 

RESEARCH EXPERIENCE 
Doctoral researcher ▪ McGill University 
Alba Guarné Group ▪ Montreal, Canada ▪ [PHONE] 

•  Designed and executed structure-function studies of DNA repair endonucleases using cryo-EM, 

X-ray crystallography and SAXS to elucidate mechanisms of recruitment and activation. 

•  Optimized  recombinant  expression  and  purification  protocols  for  >15  challenging  proteins  and 

multi-protein complexes, improving their yield more than 4-fold. 

•  Characterized  proteins  of  interest  through  DNA  binding  and  cleavage,  cross-linking,  protein-
protein interaction and thermal shift assays, to identify key residues involved in enzymatic activity. 
•  Developed optimized preparation protocols for challenging cryo-EM samples (Vitrobot, Technical 

TF20), enabling the consistent acquisition of high-quality datasets in a Titan Krios. 

•  Designed a site-specific cross-linker to increase the resolution of a protein-DNA complex from 11 

Å to 3.8 Å using cryo-EM. 

•  Led multiple structural biology projects in parallel, including a collaboration with Neophore (UK) 

on structure-guided inhibitor design, which resulted in a submission to Cell (under review). 

•  Successfully wrote applications to secure a full Ph.D. scholarship (Mexican National Council of 

Science and Technology, 2019-2024) and four additional competitive awards. 

•  Communicated complex structural biology findings at national and international venues. 
•  Supervised five undergraduate students doing research projects and trained four new graduate 
students, resulting in results in peer-reviewed publications and successful grant proposals. 

Enzymology research intern ▪ Repare Therapeutics 
Montreal, Canada ▪ [PHONE] 

•  Characterized cancer drug target proteins and evaluated small-molecule inhibitors using high-

throughput enzymatic assays (384-well format). 

•  Generated kinetic and affinity data supporting lead compound optimization by the medicinal 

chemistry team. 

•  Contributed  to  cryo-EM  structural  analysis  of  inhibitor-bound  protein  complexes,  informing 

structure-guided drug design. 

•  Designed  and  conducted  thermal  unfolding  and  spectral  shift  experiments  (Prometheus, 

Dianthus) to evaluate target potency. 

KEY SKILLS AND STRENGTHS 

•  Structural biology: Cryo-EM (VitroBot, Tecnai TF20, Titan Krios) ▪ X-ray crystallography (NT8, 
sparse matrix screens, manual tray optimization) ▪ Data collection & processing ▪ Negative stain 
EM ([CANDIDATE_NAME]) ▪ SAXS  

•  Protein science: Protein expression in E. coli & S. cerevisiae ▪ Protein purification (AKTA, IMAC, 

IEX, SEC, gravity columns) ▪ Construct design ▪ Molecular cloning ([CANDIDATE_NAME] assembly, LIC) 

	
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 •  Biophysical  techniques:  Dynamic  light  scattering  ▪  Thermal  shift  ▪  Circular  dichroism  ▪  Mass 

photometry ▪ Cross-linking MS ▪ Spectral shift 

•  Software: cryoSPARC ▪ RELION ▪ Chimera ▪ CCP4 ▪ XDS ▪ HKL2000 ▪ Phenix ▪ Coot ▪ PyMOL 
•  Personal skills:  Collaborative work ▪ Mentoring students ▪ Time management ▪ Record keeping 

▪ Experimental design ▪ Troubleshooting ▪ Protocol development 

LEADERSHIP EXPERIENCE 
President Biochemistry Graduate Student Society ▪ [PHONE] 

-  Led a team of 16 students in organizing 10+ academic, social and wellness initiatives for over 100 

biochemistry graduate students at [CANDIDATE_NAME]. 

-  Collaborated with departments across McGill to organize and fund events. 
-  Spearheaded the annual Biochemistry Research Day (80+ attendees), overseeing sponsorship, 

logistics and communication. 

EDUCATION 
Biochemistry Ph.D., Department of Biochemistry, McGill University (Canada)  [PHONE] 
Biochemistry M.Sc., Department of Biochemistry, McGill University (Canada)  [PHONE] 
Biotechnology  Engineering  B.S.,  Monterrey  Institute  of  Technology  and 
Higher Education (Mexico) 

LANGUAGES 
English (fluent), French (advanced), and Spanish (native). 

SELECTED PUBLICATIONS 
•  [CANDIDATE_NAME], J., Davis, [CANDIDATE_NAME], [CANDIDATE_NAME], H., Erie, D. A. & Guarné, A. (2025). Bacillus subtilis 
MutL samples multiple conformations during nucleotide binding and hydrolysis. Structure (Cell Press). 
•  [CANDIDATE_NAME], [CANDIDATE_NAME], [CANDIDATE_NAME], Surtees, J.A. & Guarné, A. (2025) Rad1-Rad10 uses 

different interfaces to interact with pathway-specific DNA repair factors. Pending submission. 

•  Hoemann, C. D., [CANDIDATE_NAME], J., Guzmán-Morales, [CANDIDATE_NAME], [CANDIDATE_NAME], [CANDIDATE_NAME], [CANDIDATE_NAME], [CANDIDATE_NAME], & Favis, B. D. 
immune  bioactivities  differentially  stimulate 
(2021).  [CANDIDATE_NAME]  coatings  with  distinct 
innate 
angiogenesis,  osteogenesis  and  chondrogenesis  in  poly-caprolactone  scaffolds  with  controlled 
interconnecting pore size. Bioactive Materials. 

•  Almawi, A. W., [CANDIDATE_NAME], L., Boulton, S., [CANDIDATE_NAME], J., Melacini, G., D’Amours, D., 
&  Guarné,  A.  (2020).  Distinct  surfaces  on  Cdc5/PLK  Polo-box  domain  orchestrate  combinatorial 
substrate recognition during cell division. Scientific Reports, 10(1), 1-13. 

•  Liu,  [CANDIDATE_NAME],  [CANDIDATE_NAME],  M.C.O.,  Rodríguez  González,  J.,  Pillon,  [CANDIDATE_NAME]  and  [CANDIDATE_NAME],  A.  (2019).  The 
endonuclease domain of Bacillus subtilis MutL is functionally asymmetric. DNA repair, 73, pp.1-6. 

SELECTED CONFERENCE PRESENTATIONS 
•  J. Rodríguez González, [CANDIDATE_NAME]. “The cryo-EM structure of Rad1-Rad10-Saw1 sheds light on its 
recruitment and activation mechanism in single-strand annealing”. The Protein Society 38th Annual 
Symposium. Vancouver, Canada (Poster, 2024). 

•  J. Rodríguez González, [CANDIDATE_NAME]. “Rad1-Rad10 uses different interfaces to interact with pathway-
specific DNA repair factors”. 6th DNA Repair/Replication Structures and Cancer Conference, 
[CANDIDATE_NAME], Mexico (Poster, 2024). 

•  J. Rodríguez González & A. Guarné. “Rad1-Rad10 uses different interfaces to interact with 

pathway-specific DNA repair factors”. 27th Annual Buffalo DNA Replication & Repair Symposium 
Buffalo, University at Buffalo, NY, USA (Talk & poster, October 2023). 

•  J. Rodríguez González & A. Guarné. “Recruitment and activation of Rad1-Rad10 during single-

strand annealing”. 6th Annual CRBS Symposium, Montreal, QC, Canada. (Best poster 
presentation award, 2024).
pitch_text:
[EXCEL SOURCE]: R&D Engineer/ Scientist​, R&D Manager​, Business Operations Analyst​, Scientific Consultant​, QC/ QA manager​ 


[FILE: clarify_JavierRodriguezG.pdf]
Document 1: Assignment 1 (Clarify) 

Question #1: My Ikigai 

My Ikigai is visualizing molecular mechanisms to accelerate drug discovery. 

The central theme of my career is "Clarity." My passion lies in solving the 3D puzzles of 
life—taking a chaotic mixture of proteins and resolving them into a high-resolution structure that 
explains how they work. My talent is technical mastery over the tools that make this possible 
(Cryo-EM, X-ray crystallography) and the patience to optimize challenging protein samples. 

The world needs faster, more precise treatments for diseases like cancer. By intersecting my 
structural biology expertise with the pharmaceutical industry's need for Structure-Guided Drug 
Design (SGDD), I create a lasting impact: providing the "molecular maps" that medicinal 
chemists need to build better drugs. To align my career with this, I am fully pivoting from 
academia to the biotech sector (companies like Amgen or AbCellera), where I can apply my 
skills to real-world therapeutic targets rather than just theoretical models. 

Question #2: My Ideal Day 

My ideal day is a hybrid of computational discovery and collaborative science. 

It starts by checking the results of an overnight data collection on the Titan Krios. I spend the 
morning processing this data using cryoSPARC or RELION, feeling the excitement as a 2D 
class average sharpens into a 3D volume. 

+1 

Lunch is shared with a cross-functional team—medicinal chemists and biologists—where we 
discuss how my structural data explains the kinetic results from a recent thermal shift assay. In 
the afternoon, I might troubleshoot a protein purification on the AKTA system to improve the 
yield of a new target. The day ends by mentoring a junior scientist, reviewing their experimental 
design, ensuring we are moving the project forward efficiently. 

Question #3: Job Titles 

1.  Scientist, Structural Biology: This is my direct target (e.g., Amgen). It utilizes my core 
expertise in Cryo-EM and crystallography to solve protein-drug complex structures. 
+1 

2.  R&D Scientist (Protein Biochemistry): I have a proven track record of optimizing 

expression for over 15 challenging proteins. This role focuses on the "upstream" supply 
of high-quality samples. 

3.  Scientific Consultant: With my broad background (PhD + Industry Internship) and 

trilingual skills (English/French/Spanish), I can advise firms on the feasibility of structural 
biology projects. 

 4.  Business Operations Analyst (Biotech): My leadership experience as President of the 
Biochemistry Graduate Student Society gave me a taste for the operational and strategic 
side of science, which I find appealing for a future career pivot. 

Question #4: S.M.A.R.T Goals 

●  Goal 1 (Immediate Career): Secure a Scientist position at a major biotech firm (Target: 

Amgen, AbCellera, or [CANDIDATE_NAME]) by April 2026. 

○  Measurable: Secure 3 interviews per month; follow up on my current application 

to Amgen Burnaby within 1 week. 

○  Attainable: I am already in the pipeline and have relevant internship experience. 

●  Goal 2 (Scientific Output): Publish my collaborative paper with Neophore in Cell 

(currently under review) by June 2026. 

○  Measurable: Manuscript accepted and indexed. 
○  Realistic: The work is submitted; primarily requires responding to reviewer 

comments. 

●  Goal 3 (Network Expansion): Connect with 15 scientists in the Vancouver/British 
Columbia biotech hub over the next 3 months to support my relocation plans. 

○  Measurable: 5 virtual coffee chats or informational interviews. 
○  Time-bound: By March 30, 2026. 
○ 

 
 
 

[FILE: pitch_JavierRodriguezG.pdf]
Document 2: Assignment 4 (Pitch) 

Selected Job Offer Position: Scientist - Structural Biology (Amgen - Burnaby, BC) 
Key Requirements: 

●  PhD in Structural Biology or Biophysics. 
●  Expertise in Cryo-EM data collection and processing. 
●  Experience in protein purification and biochemical assays. 
●  Ability to work in cross-functional drug discovery teams. 

The Elevator Pitch 

"Hi, [CANDIDATE_NAME]. I am a Structural Biologist who specializes in the 'impossible' targets. 

During my PhD at [CANDIDATE_NAME], I didn't just solve structures; I developed protocols for proteins 
that others struggled to purify. I used Cryo-EM to take a DNA-repair complex from a 
low-resolution blob to a 3.8 Å structure, using a custom site-specific cross-linker I 
designed myself. 

But I’m not just an academic. I spent this past summer at Repare Therapeutics, where 
I worked directly in the drug discovery pipeline. I know how to speak the language of 
medicinal chemists, generating the kinetic and thermal shift data needed to validate 
your lead compounds. 

I’ve applied to your team at Amgen because you are leaders in biologics, and I want to 
apply my dual expertise in high-resolution structure determination and 
high-throughput biophysics to accelerate your next generation of therapeutics."
